slides.title: Unit 1
slides.subtitle: Introduction to Basic Computing
slides.notes: Teach Unit 1 as a modular block (6 hours).

slides.title: Unit 1 — Introduction to Basic Computing
slides.notes: Six teaching hours, 10% weight. Emphasize legal workflows: research, secure files, professional email. AI is introduced at survey level; ethics deepens in Unit 5.

slides.title: What Is a Computer?
bullets: Electronic device that processes data into information
Follows instructions (programs) stored in software
Transforms raw facts into usable legal information
Used daily for drafting, research, and communication
Combines hardware, software, and human operators
Foundation for all other units in this course
slides.notes: Define computer clearly for short-answer exams. Stress the data-to-information transformation—lawyers work with facts, dates, parties, and citations as data.

slides.title: Computer System Components

left.heading: Hardware
bullets: CPU and RAM — run Word, Excel, browsers
Storage — SSD, cloud, USB for case files
Input — keyboard, scanner for petitions
Output — monitor, printer for filings

right.heading: Software & People
bullets: System software — operating system (OS)
Application software — Word, Excel, research tools
Peopleware — users following professional discipline
IPO cycle: Input → Process → Output → Storage

slides.notes: Use the IPO model. Hardware is physical; software is instructions. Do not treat Office apps as the only software—the OS and browser matter too.

slides.title: Operating Systems — Core Functions
bullets: System software managing hardware and resources
Boots the machine and runs application programs
Organizes files, folders, users, and permissions
Examples: Windows, macOS, Linux
Provides security accounts and device drivers
Platform for all legal applications you will use
slides.notes: OS is high-yield for exams. Two functions to memorize: resource management and providing a platform for applications. Tie to folder discipline for client matters.

slides.title: OS Skills for Legal Practice
bullets: Folder structure: Client / Case / Year hierarchy
Clear file naming: year, parties, doc type, version
Backups before court deadlines and submissions
Respect permissions on shared chambers drives
Separate drafts from final filed PDFs
Never store sole copies on unencrypted USB sticks
slides.notes: Demonstrate a sample path like Research/Judgments/2026/. Professional reliability starts with OS-level file discipline, not just Word formatting.

slides.title: Internet Basics for Lawyers
bullets: Global network linking computers and services worldwide
Browser accesses websites and authenticated databases
Search engines find open web; databases hold primary law
Cloud services store and sync documents remotely
HTTPS and logins protect access to paid research tools
Download, rename, and log sources in research folders
slides.notes: Walk through a judgment-download workflow: OS file manager → browser → database → save PDF with clear name. Distinguish open web from subscription legal databases.

slides.title: Digital Communication Channels
bullets: Email — formal advocate and chambers correspondence
Messaging and video calls for client meetings
Shared drives for team document collaboration
Clear subject lines and labeled attachments
Confidentiality: avoid personal channels for client data
Use institution-approved tools where required
slides.notes: Email remains professional default. Teach subject-line clarity and attachment naming. Warn against sending sensitive matter details through unsecured personal apps.

slides.title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
bullets: Systems performing tasks needing human-like intelligence
Pattern recognition, language understanding, recommendations
Already embedded in research and document tools
Not limited to chatbots — includes analytics dashboards
Assists lawyers; does not replace professional judgment
Ethical use is mandatory — detailed in Unit 5
slides.notes: Counter the myth that AI equals only ChatGPT. Frame AI as a family of techniques applied across legal tech. Preview that Unit 5 covers ethics in depth.

slides.title: Brief History of AI
bullets: 1950s–80s: symbolic and rule-based expert systems
1990s–2000s: machine learning from data examples
2010s: deep learning advances in language and vision
2020s: generative AI widely available to professionals
Legal tech adoption accelerated with NLP tools
Regulation and ethics now catch up with capability
slides.notes: Keep history brief—a timeline slide for exams. Key exam phrase: evolution from rules → learning from data → generative models.

slides.title: AI in the Legal Profession

left.heading: Current Applications
bullets: Legal research and case summarization
Document review and contract analysis support
Analytics dashboards for case management
Drafting assistance under lawyer supervision

right.heading: Professional Caution
bullets: Verify every citation — AI can hallucinate
Protect client confidentiality in all tools
Human lawyer remains accountable
Unit 5 covers ethics and limitations fully

slides.notes: High-yield short note topic: AI applications in law. Always pair capability with accountability. Hallucinated citations are a known professional risk.

slides.title: AI Techniques and Future Scope
bullets: Expert systems — rules encoding legal logic (conceptual)
Machine learning — models trained on examples
NLP — processing statutes, judgments, contracts
Generative models — create new text from patterns
Future: more embedded tools, stronger analytics
Growing demand for ethical and regulatory frameworks
slides.notes: Conceptual only—no math required. Students should name technique families and link each to a legal use case at a survey level.

slides.title: Unit 1 — Exam Tips
bullets: Define computer and explain IPO + storage cycle
List hardware vs application vs system software
State two core functions of an operating system
Distinguish browser search from legal databases
Short note: AI applications in the legal profession
Avoid confusing OS with MS Office applications
slides.notes: Exam tip slide. Common MCQ traps: software categories, OS purpose, and over-trusting AI. Revise definitions and one legal workflow example.

slides.title: Unit 1 — Next steps
bullets: Complete unit labs
Attempt unit Q&A bank
Submit unit assignment
Revise using revision sheet
slides.notes: Point students to materials/labs and materials/qa for this unit.
